events.text: Patient is a 76-year-old White male with a 55 pack-year smoking history (former smoker, quit 1 year ago after diagnosis), ECOG performance status 2, BMI 20.3.

At age 76, patient was diagnosed with non-small cell lung cancer, squamous cell carcinoma (histology 8070), primary site left lower lobe (C34.3), clinical stage IVA, T4N3M1a, with contralateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy and contralateral pulmonary nodule.

At age 76, patient presented with worsening cough, hemoptysis, and significant fatigue over 2 months. Weight loss of 25 pounds. Decreased appetite and performance status decline. Multiple comorbidities including COPD, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes.

At age 76, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated a 7.2 x 6.1 cm mass in the left lower lobe encasing the left main bronchus with complete left lower lobe atelectasis. Bulky bilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy, left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy measuring 1.8 cm. Single 1.2 cm right lower lobe pulmonary nodule. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases.

At age 76, PET/CT showed intensely hypermetabolic left lower lobe mass (SUVmax 22.4) with left lower lobe atelectasis, bilateral mediastinal (SUVmax 11.6), left supraclavicular (SUVmax 8.3), and contralateral right lower lobe nodule (SUVmax 5.7). No extrathoracic distant disease.

At age 76, bronchoscopic biopsy of left lower lobe endobronchial mass revealed poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with necrosis. Immunohistochemistry: p40 positive, CK5/6 positive, TTF-1 negative. PD-L1 TPS 90% (22C3 assay).

At age 76, next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT) of tumor specimen: EGFR wild-type, ALK rearrangement not detected, ROS1 rearrangement not detected, TP53 nonsense mutation detected, PIK3CA amplification detected, SOX2 amplification detected, FGFR1 amplification detected. No actionable driver mutations. Tumor mutational burden 18.6 mut/Mb. Microsatellite stable.

At age 76, multidisciplinary tumor board: stage IVA squamous cell carcinoma with very high PD-L1 TPS 90% and high TMB. Given ECOG PS 2 and multiple comorbidities, pembrolizumab monotherapy recommended over combination chemoimmunotherapy to limit toxicity.
At age 76, initiated pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks as first-line monotherapy for metastatic squamous NSCLC with high PD-L1 expression.
At age 76, after cycle 1 of pembrolizumab, patient developed grade 2 fatigue requiring increased rest periods. Grade 1 decreased appetite continued.
At age 76, after cycle 3 of pembrolizumab, patient developed grade 1 diarrhea (3 loose stools daily) and grade 1 pruritic rash on upper extremities.

At age 76, restaging CT after 4 cycles of pembrolizumab showed partial response. Left lower lobe mass decreased to 5.0 x 4.2 cm with partial re-aeration of the left lower lobe. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy decreased. Right lower lobe nodule decreased to 0.7 cm.

At age 76, encouraging partial response to pembrolizumab monotherapy. Patient reports improvement in cough, hemoptysis resolved, appetite slightly improved. ECOG performance status improved from 2 to 1.
At age 76, continued pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks.

At age 77, restaging CT after 8 cycles showed continued response. Left lower lobe mass now 3.6 x 3.1 cm. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy significantly reduced. Right lower lobe nodule barely visible at 0.4 cm.
At age 77, after cycle 10, patient developed grade 2 colitis with 5-6 loose stools daily and mild abdominal cramping. Pembrolizumab held. Started budesonide 9 mg daily.
At age 77, immune-related colitis managed with budesonide. Symptoms improved within 1 week. Transitioned to budesonide taper over 4 weeks. Pembrolizumab held for total of 6 weeks.
At age 77, resumed pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks after resolution of colitis.
At age 77, restaging CT after treatment gap showed continued partial response. No evidence of progression during treatment hold.
At age 77, patient continues on pembrolizumab with durable response. Colitis has not recurred. Weight stabilized. ECOG PS 1 maintained. Plan to continue treatment with ongoing surveillance.

scenario_blurb: Stage IIIA-IV non-small cell lung cancer without actionable driver mutations (EGFR wild-type, ALK negative, ROS1 negative). Treated with carboplatin/pemetrexed plus pembrolizumab immunotherapy. Mix of adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma. Some with high PD-L1 expression receiving pembrolizumab monotherapy.
